title: Sticky toffee pudding cake
subtitle: A date sponge baked in a tin, served with toffee sauce
excerpt: A moist date sponge baked in a square tin and served warm with a dark toffee sauce poured over. The dates dissolve into the batter and add sweetness rather than texture. Cuts into nine squares.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding is documented as a British restaurant dessert from the 1970s. Francis Coulson of the Sharrow Bay Country House Hotel is widely credited with popularising the dish in its current form. The recipe draws on the Sharrow Bay recipe as described in British food journalism and the household form widely reproduced in British home-baking guides from the 1980s onwards. No single public-domain text predating the twentieth century documents this combination.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked sponge without the sauce. Reheat from frozen at 160°C fan for 15 minutes. Make the sauce fresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge can be baked a day ahead and reheated in the tin, covered in foil, at 160°C fan for 20 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking chopped dried dates in boiling water with a little bicarbonate of soda before adding them to a batter. The hot water softens the dates to a near-paste and the alkali from the bicarbonate breaks down their cell structure, so they dissolve almost entirely into the cake during baking. The result is a moist, intensely flavoured sponge with no chewy date pieces.

content.text: before the batter is made. This step dissolves them almost completely and is the reason the finished cake is so moist. Do not skip it. The sponge looks like a plain, barely-sweet cake until the sauce goes on; that is correct.
Bloom the dates and make the batter

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line a 23 cm square tin. Place the chopped {{dates-medjool}} in a heatproof bowl with the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and pour over 250 ml boiling water. Stir and leave for 10 minutes; the dates will soften to a rough paste.

Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} (sponge amount) with the {{dark-brown-sugar}} (sponge amount) until combined. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}} and {{vanilla-extract}}, then stir in the date mixture — water and all. Pour into the tin.
Bake and make the sauce — 30–35 minutes

Bake for 30–35 minutes, until the sponge is risen, set across the surface, and a skewer comes out clean. While it bakes, combine the {{unsalted-butter}} (sauce amount), {{dark-brown-sugar}} (sauce amount), {{double-cream}}, and {{black-treacle}} in a saucepan and heat gently, stirring, until the sugar is dissolved and the sauce is smooth and slightly thickened, about 5 minutes. Serve the warm sponge cut into squares with the hot toffee sauce poured over.
